title: Thinking about your thinking
description: Plan, monitor, and check your own learning — the skill behind every skill.
summaryPoints: Run every study session in three phases: plan, monitor, evaluate
Spot the illusion of competence — recognizing isn't the same as recalling
Self-test to expose gaps; the harder it feels, the more it's working
Use the Feynman trick: explain it simply, and your fuzzy spots show up
Ask 'how do I know I actually know this?' before you call it learned

content.title: The voice in your head
content.body:
Ever finish reading a page, glance up, and realize you have no idea what you just read? That little nudge — wait, did I actually get that? — is your brain checking on itself.

That's **metacognition**: thinking about your own thinking. It's the quiet skill behind every other skill, because it's how you steer your learning instead of just hoping it sticks.
content.imageAlt: A brain reflected in a mirror, thinking about thinking

content.title: Three phases, every time
content.body:
Good learners run a loop, usually without noticing:
- **Plan** — What do I already know? What's the goal? Which strategy fits?
- **Monitor** — Is this working? Do I really get it, or do I just recognize it?
- **Evaluate** — What worked? What would I change next time?

Most people only ever do the middle part, and badly. The real wins hide in the planning up front and the honest check at the end.

content.prompt: Put the metacognition loop in the order you actually use it.
steps: Plan: figure out the goal and pick a strategy
Monitor: check mid-session whether it's working
Evaluate: ask what worked and what to change
content.explanation: Plan before you start, monitor while you go, evaluate after. Skip the first and last and you're just flailing in the middle.

content.prompt: Match each phase to the question it asks.

pairs.right: What would I change next time?

content.title: The trap: feeling like you know
content.body:
Here's the sneaky part. Rereading your notes and slapping highlighter everywhere feels productive. The words get familiar, so your brain whispers, "Yeah, I've got this."

That warm feeling is the **illusion of competence**. Recognizing something on the page is not the same as being able to pull it out of your head in an exam — where the page isn't there to help.

content.prompt: If you reread something until it feels familiar, you've learned it.
content.explanation: Familiarity is recognition, not recall. Feeling like you know it is exactly the illusion of competence the exam will expose.

content.instructions: One word makes this false. Tap it.
content.sentence: Highlighting and rereading are the best proof that you truly know something.
content.explanation: Rereading and highlighting feel productive but only build familiarity — self-testing is what proves real recall.

content.prompt: Feels like learning, or actually builds it?

items.text: Closing the book and writing what you remember
Rereading your notes a fifth time
Quizzing yourself with the page hidden
Copying the slides out word for word
Explaining it out loud from memory

content.title: Self-testing breaks the spell
content.body:
The fix is uncomfortable on purpose: **close the book and test yourself.**

When you try to recall an answer and come up blank, that blank is the truth. It feels harder than rereading — and that difficulty is the whole point. The struggle is your brain actually building the memory, and it instantly shows you the gaps you'd otherwise miss.

content.prompt: What actually tells you whether you understand something?
content.context: You've read the chapter three times and it all looks familiar.
options: Reread it a fourth time, slower
Highlight the parts that felt important
Close the book and try to explain or test yourself on it
Notice that none of it surprised you
content.explanation: Only retrieval — pulling it out without the page — proves real understanding. Everything else just measures familiarity.

content.title: Explain it to a twelve-year-old
content.body:
The **Feynman technique** is a brutal honesty machine. Pick the idea and explain it in plain words, as if to a curious twelve-year-old — no jargon to hide behind.

Wherever you get stuck, vague, or reach for a fancy term you can't unpack? That's exactly what you don't truly understand yet. Go relearn that spot, then try the explanation again.

content.prompt: In the Feynman technique, what does getting stuck or vague reveal?
options: That the topic is just naturally confusing
The precise spot you don't actually understand yet
That you should use more technical terms
content.explanation: Stumbling is a feature, not a flaw — it pinpoints the exact gap so you know what to go relearn.

content.prompt: The honest gut-check before calling something learned: How do I know I actually ___ this?
content.explanation: "How do I know I actually know this?" forces real evidence — a test passed, an idea explained — instead of a vague feeling.

content.prompt: Weaker students tend to overestimate how much they actually know.
content.explanation: The less you know, the harder it is to see what's missing. Practice tests calibrate that gap so your confidence matches reality.

content.prompt: You feel super confident but want to be sure. What calibrates that confidence best?
options: Taking a practice test under real conditions
Reading your notes one more time
Asking a friend if they think you're ready
content.explanation: A practice test shows your true score, not your gut feeling — the cure for overestimating what you know.

content.prompt: Pick a real task coming up. Write your PLAN — what you already know, the goal, and the strategy you'll use — then how you'll CHECK it's actually working.
content.context: This is the whole loop in one move: plan it, then build in a way to monitor yourself instead of just hoping.
content.placeholder: I already know… my goal is… my strategy is… I'll know it's working when…
content.explanation: Naming the goal, the strategy, and the check turns vague studying into something you can actually steer.
